op.name: fft.ihfft
op.notes: Inverse FFT of Hermitian signal. Cost: 5*n*ceil(log2(n)) (Cooley-Tukey radix-2; Van Loan 1992 §1.4).
op.summary: Compute the inverse FFT of a signal that has Hermitian symmetry.

blocks.text: Compute the inverse FFT of a signal that has Hermitian symmetry.

desc_blocks.text:
Length of the inverse FFT, the number of points along
transformation axis in the input to use.  If `n` is smaller than
the length of the input, the input is cropped.  If it is larger,
the input is padded with zeros. If `n` is not given, the length of
the input along the axis specified by `axis` is used.

Axis over which to compute the inverse FFT. If not given, the last
axis is used.

Normalization mode (see `numpy.fft`). Default is "backward".
Indicates which direction of the forward/backward pair of transforms
is scaled and with what normalization factor.

If provided, the result will be placed in this array. It should be
of the appropriate shape and dtype.

The truncated or zero-padded input, transformed along the axis
indicated by `axis`, or the last one if `axis` is not specified.
The length of the transformed axis is ``n//2 + 1``.

blocks.text:
`hfft`/`ihfft` are a pair analogous to `rfft`/`irfft`, but for the
opposite case: here the signal has Hermitian symmetry in the time
domain and is real in the frequency domain. So here it's `hfft` for
which you must supply the length of the result if it is to be odd:

* even: ``ihfft(hfft(a, 2*len(a) - 2)) == a``, within roundoff error,
* odd: ``ihfft(hfft(a, 2*len(a) - 1)) == a``, within roundoff error.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> spectrum = np.array([ 15, -4, 0, -1, 0, -4])
>>> np.fft.ifft(spectrum)
array([1.+0.j,  2.+0.j,  3.+0.j,  4.+0.j,  3.+0.j,  2.+0.j]) # may vary
>>> np.fft.ihfft(spectrum)
array([ 1.-0.j,  2.-0.j,  3.-0.j,  4.-0.j]) # may vary
